title: Chapter 13 - Accounting for Revenue (SLFRS 15)
description: Examines the five-step revenue recognition model under SLFRS 15, focusing on conceptual application and analysis of contracts, performance obligations, transaction price, and allocation.
subject: ACC1370 - Financial Accounting and Reporting

questions.question: An entity enters a contract to sell a product and provide a mandatory, highly specialized installation service that integrates the product into the customer's unique network, making the product unusable without the service. According to SLFRS 15, what is the most appropriate accounting treatment for the performance obligations?
options: The product and the service are two separate performance obligations because they are two distinct promises.
The product and the service must be combined and accounted for as a single performance obligation because the promises are interdependent and interrelated.
The product revenue is recognized at a point in time, and the service revenue is recognized over time.
The service is an input to produce the product, so only the product is a performance obligation.
questions.answer: The product and the service must be combined and accounted for as a single performance obligation because the promises are interdependent and interrelated.
questions.rationale: If promised goods or services are not distinct, meaning they are interdependent and interrelated, they are combined and reported as one performance obligation. Highly specialized, essential installation indicates the promises are not distinct.

questions.question: What is the primary factor that determines when revenue is recognized according to the final step of the SLFRS 15 model?
options: The collection of cash from the customer.
The signing date of the contract by all parties.
The entity satisfying a performance obligation by transferring control of the promised good or service to the customer.
The date when the entity transfers legal title to the customer.
questions.answer: The entity satisfying a performance obligation by transferring control of the promised good or service to the customer.
questions.rationale: Revenue is recognized when the entity satisfies a performance obligation by transferring a promised good or service, which occurs when the customer obtains control of that good or service.

questions.question: A company sells a machine for Rs 500,000 on 1.1.2021, with payment due on 31.12.2023. The standalone selling price is Rs 400,000. Assuming a significant financing component, what is the Rs 100,000 difference most accurately representing in the context of revenue recognition?
options: A volume discount that will be recognized over the contract period.
Interest revenue that should be recognized over the two-year period.
Variable consideration that must be estimated at contract inception.
An allocation of transaction price to a separate performance obligation.
questions.answer: Interest revenue that should be recognized over the two-year period.
questions.rationale: If the payment interval is significant, the difference between the payment amount and the fair value/standalone price includes a significant financing component (interest), which must be adjusted for the time value of money and recognized as interest revenue over time.

questions.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT a required criterion for a customer contract to exist under Step 1 of the SLFRS 15 model?
options: The parties have approved the contract and are committed to performing their obligations.
It is probable that the entity will collect the consideration it is entitled to.
The contract price is fixed and does not include any variable consideration.
The entity can identify each party's rights regarding the goods or services to be transferred.
questions.answer: The contract price is fixed and does not include any variable consideration.
questions.rationale: The transaction price can include variable consideration; therefore, a fixed price is not a required criterion. The other options are explicit criteria for a contract to exist.

questions.question: According to the transaction price allocation step (Step 4), how should a discount included in the total consideration of a contract with multiple performance obligations generally be allocated?
options: Allocated entirely to the performance obligation that generated the highest margin.
Allocated entirely to the final performance obligation to be satisfied.
Allocated among all performance obligations on a relative stand-alone selling price basis (proportionate allocation).
It is recognized as a reduction of Cost of Goods Sold and not allocated.
questions.answer: Allocated among all performance obligations on a relative stand-alone selling price basis (proportionate allocation).
questions.rationale: Any overall discount is allocated between the performance obligations on a relative stand-alone selling price basis (Proportionate allocation) unless another basis provides reliable allocation.

questions.question: A contract requires a customer to pay Rs 10,000 upon delivery and an additional Rs 5,000 if the entity achieves a specified customer satisfaction score within 3 months. The entity has no experience with this type of performance bonus but estimates a 60% chance of achieving the bonus. Assuming the entity uses the expected value method, how should the variable consideration be treated in determining the transaction price?
options: The Rs 5,000 must be included in the transaction price immediately.
The Rs 5,000 is included only if the entity is highly probable that there will not be a significant reversal of previously recognized revenue.
The Rs 5,000 is excluded because the entity lacks sufficient experience.
Rs 3,000 (60% of Rs 5,000) is included and recognized as revenue.
questions.answer: The Rs 5,000 is included only if the entity is highly probable that there will not be a significant reversal of previously recognized revenue.
questions.rationale: Variable consideration is included in the transaction price only if the entity has experience with a similar contract AND it is highly probable that there will not be a significant reversal of previously recognized revenue. The lack of experience suggests a significant reversal is possible.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the difference between a Contract Asset and a Trade Receivable?
options: A Trade Receivable is always current, while a Contract Asset is always non-current.
A Trade Receivable's right to consideration is unconditional; a Contract Asset's right is conditional on something other than the passage of time.
A Contract Asset is measured at fair value, while a Trade Receivable is measured at the transaction price.
A Contract Asset only arises in contracts with multiple performance obligations.
questions.answer: A Trade Receivable's right to consideration is unconditional; a Contract Asset's right is conditional on something other than the passage of time.
questions.rationale: A Contract Asset is an entity's right to consideration conditioned on something other than the passage of time (e.g., fulfilling another performance obligation). An unconditional right is presented separately as a receivable (e.g., Trade Receivable).

questions.question: Under SLFRS 15, when an entity receives non-cash consideration for a transfer of goods or services, how should the revenue generally be recognized?
options: At the book value of the non-cash consideration received.
At the contractually agreed-upon cash price, ignoring the non-cash item's value.
At the fair value of what is received.
At the entity's cost to produce the goods or services transferred.
questions.answer: At the fair value of what is received.
questions.rationale: When non-cash consideration is received, the revenue should generally be recognized at the fair value of what is received.

questions.question: In the case of a long-term service contract, when is the performance obligation satisfied and revenue recognized?
options: At a single point in time when the final result of the service is delivered.
Over time, by selecting an appropriate method for measuring the entity's progress towards completion.
Only when the entity has a present right to receive payment for the service.
At the contract inception date, as the service commitment is established.
questions.answer: Over time, by selecting an appropriate method for measuring the entity's progress towards completion.
questions.rationale: When the service is long-term, the performance obligation is satisfied over time by selecting an appropriate method for measuring the entity’s progress (e.g., long-term construction contracts).

questions.question: A software company sells a software license for Rs 100,000 and provides one year of technical support, with a total contract price of Rs 120,000. The standalone selling price of the license is Rs 100,000, and the standalone selling price of the support is Rs 30,000. How should the transaction price be allocated?
options: License Rs 100,000; Support Rs 20,000 (Based on total contract price).
License Rs 92,308; Support Rs 27,692 (Based on relative standalone selling prices).
License Rs 100,000; Support Rs 30,000 (Based on standalone prices, with a discount recognized in COGS).
License Rs 80,000; Support Rs 40,000 (Based on a 4:1 ratio of standalone prices).
questions.answer: License Rs 92,308; Support Rs 27,692 (Based on relative standalone selling prices).
questions.rationale: The total standalone price is 130,000. The discount is Rs 10,000 (130,000-120,000). The allocation ratio for the License is 100/130 (76.92%), and for Support is 30/130 (23.08%). License: 120,000 * 76.92% = Rs 92,308. Support: 120,000 * 23.08% = Rs 27,692.

questions.question: What is the key indicator that a customer has obtained control of an asset, allowing the entity to recognize revenue at a point in time?
options: The customer has made a partial cash payment.
The entity has a present right to receive the payment for the asset.
The entity has transferred physical possession of the asset but retained legal title.
The contract is for the sale of goods rather than services.
questions.answer: The entity has a present right to receive the payment for the asset.
questions.rationale: The entity having a present right to receive payment for the asset is a factor indicating that control is passed at a point in time. The transfer of control is the critical concept.

questions.question: A Contract Liability is created when a customer pays a company Rs 50,000 in advance for products that will be delivered next month. What is the fundamental accounting concept represented by this liability?
options: An entity's right to consideration for goods transferred.
An entity's obligation to transfer goods or services for which consideration has been received.
Unconditional right to a future cash flow from a customer.
A cost incurred to fulfill a contract that is expected to be recovered.
questions.answer: An entity's obligation to transfer goods or services for which consideration has been received.
questions.rationale: A Contract Liability is an entity's obligation to transfer goods or services to a customer for which the entity has received consideration. It is often called unearned revenue.

questions.question: In the context of Step 3 (Determine the Transaction Price), for which situation is it *not* practically required to adjust the transaction price for the effects of the time value of money?
options: The customer pays the consideration in advance, and the contract period is 36 months.
The customer pays in arrears, and the interval between transfer and payment is expected to be 15 months.
The interval between the transfer of goods and customer payment is expected to be 10 months.
The contract is a long-term construction contract satisfied over 3 years.
questions.answer: The interval between the transfer of goods and customer payment is expected to be 10 months.
questions.rationale: Practically, when the interval between the transfer of promised goods or services and the payment by the customer is expected to be less than 12 months, it does not require adjusting for the effects of the time value of money.

questions.question: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract, such as sales commissions, must be recognized as an asset and amortized UNLESS:
options: The commissions are paid to external third-party agents.
The entity does not expect to recover the costs.
The amortization period would be one year or less.
The contract contains variable consideration.
questions.answer: The amortization period would be one year or less.
questions.rationale: If the amortization period would be one year or less, the incremental costs of obtaining a contract can be expensed (as a practical expedient) instead of recognized as an asset and amortized.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between Income and Revenue as defined in the lecture materials?
options: Income results from an increase in assets or decrease in liabilities; Revenue is an income from the ordinary course of business.
Income is always a fixed amount; Revenue is always variable.
Income is recognized only at a point in time; Revenue is recognized only over time.
Income is recognized under SLFRS 15; Revenue is recognized under LKAS 18.
questions.answer: Income results from an increase in assets or decrease in liabilities; Revenue is an income from the ordinary course of business.
questions.rationale: Income is a broader concept (increase in assets or decrease in liabilities that results in an increase in equity except for transactions with Equity holders). Revenue is a specific type of income arising from the ordinary course of business.

questions.question: A promise to supply and [dropdown_1] a sophisticated machine like a power plant is typically combined into [dropdown_2] performance obligation because the promises are interdependent and interrelated.

options: install
sell
maintain
finance
two
a single
three
multiple

questions.rationale: A promise to supply and install a sophisticated machine is an example where the promises are not distinct and are combined into a single performance obligation, often satisfied over time.

questions.question: The SLFRS 15 standard supersedes LKAS 18 (Revenue) and [dropdown_1] (Construction Contracts). The overall objective of SLFRS 15 is to provide information about the nature, amount, timing, and [dropdown_2] of revenue and cash flows.

options: LKAS 11
LKAS 16
LKAS 2
SLFRS 9
certainty
profitability
uncertainty
source

questions.rationale: SLFRS 15 supersedes LKAS 11 Construction Contracts and LKAS 18 Revenue. The objective includes establishing principles for reporting information about the nature, amount, timing, and uncertainty of revenue and cash flows.

questions.question: If a contract contains many performance obligations, the entity must allocate the transaction price by reference to their relative [dropdown_1]. If this price is not directly observable, the entity must [dropdown_2] it.

options: fair market values
standalone selling prices
cost plus margin
negotiated prices
ignore
estimate
renegotiate
defer

questions.rationale: If there are many performance obligations, the transaction price is allocated by reference to their relative stand-alone selling prices. If not directly observable, the entity must estimate it.

questions.question: The second step in the five-step revenue model is to identify the ________ in the contract.
questions.rationale: The five steps are 1. Identify the contract, 2. Identify the performance obligations, 3. Determine the transaction price, 4. Allocate the transaction price, 5. Recognize revenue.

questions.question: A customer purchasing more than 1,000 units in a year, leading to a reduced price per unit for all purchases, is an example of ________ consideration.
questions.rationale: Variable consideration is consideration of a contract that depends on future events (e.g., Discounts; Refunds; Price concessions; Performance bonuses; Rebates; Credits; Incentives; and Penalties).

questions.question: The combination of an entity's rights to consideration and its performance obligations under a contract gives rise to a net asset or a net ________ in the Statement of Financial Position.
questions.rationale: The combination of those rights and performance obligations gives rise to an asset (net) or a liability (net) on the Statement of Financial Position (e.g., Contract Asset or Contract Liability).

questions.question: Select all the factors that may indicate the transfer of control of a good to a customer at a point in time, allowing revenue recognition.
options: The entity has a present right to receive payment for the asset.
The entity has transferred legal title to the asset.
The contract has commercial substance.
The customer has significant risks and rewards related to the ownership of the asset.
answer: The entity has a present right to receive payment for the asset.
The entity has transferred legal title to the asset.
The customer has significant risks and rewards related to the ownership of the asset.
questions.rationale: Factors indicating control is passed at a point in time include the entity's present right to payment, transfer of legal title, transfer of physical possession, transfer of significant risks and rewards, and customer acceptance. Commercial substance is a contract existence criterion, not a control transfer factor.

questions.question: Which of the following costs incurred to obtain or fulfill a contract must be recognized as an asset?
options: Commissions paid to sales employees for a 3-year contract.
Costs for IT equipment that will be used exclusively to service a single 3-year contract, expected to be recovered.
External legal fees for due diligence on a contract.
Incremental costs of obtaining a contract with an expected amortization period of 8 months.
answer: Commissions paid to sales employees for a 3-year contract.
Costs for IT equipment that will be used exclusively to service a single 3-year contract, expected to be recovered.
questions.rationale: Commissions for a multi-year contract are incremental costs of obtaining the contract and should be recognized as an asset and amortized. IT equipment specifically used for the contract meets the criteria for 'Costs to Fulfill a Contract' (relate directly, generate/enhance resources, expected to be recovered). Legal fees are generally expensed as they are not incremental to obtaining the contract, and the 8-month incremental cost can be expensed under the practical expedient.

questions.question: According to SLFRS 15, which of the following accounting standards and interpretations were superseded by its implementation?
options: LKAS 11 Construction Contracts
LKAS 18 Revenue
IFRIC 13 Customer Loyalty Programme
SLFRS 9 Financial Instruments
questions.rationale: SLFRS 15 supersedes LKAS 11, LKAS 18, IFRIC 13, IFRIC 15, IFRIC 18, and SIC-31. SLFRS 9 remains in force for Financial Instruments.

questions.question: Match each contract term to its appropriate presentation in the Statement of Financial Position.

matchPairs.left: Advance received for a future sale
Unconditional right to receive payment upon transfer of goods
Right to payment is conditional upon completion of a different contract obligation

questions.question: Match the revenue model step with its corresponding conceptual focus.

matchPairs.right: Determining if promises are distinct or combined
Estimating variable consideration or non-cash value
Assessing transfer of control (point in time or over time)

questions.question: Match each variable consideration component with its description.

matchPairs.right: A reduction in price for purchases
A reduction in consideration for failure to meet a contract term
An additional amount payable if certain targets are met

questions.question: A consulting firm enters a contract to deliver a standard IT audit report (which is routinely performed by others) and provide 6 months of customized, specialized post-audit consulting that requires extensive, unique knowledge of the firm's systems. Apply the SLFRS 15 Step 2 criteria to analyze whether these two promises constitute a single or separate performance obligation(s) and justify your conclusion.

Explain the qualitative and quantitative disclosure requirements that an entity must provide under SLFRS 15, specifically focusing on the information related to significant judgments made by management.

Describe the three criteria that must ALL be met for an entity to capitalize 'Costs to Fulfill a Contract' as an asset, and provide an example of such a cost that would meet these criteria, contrasting it with a cost that would not.
